attributes.stem: A 55-year-old woman is undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anaesthesia. She has a history of well-controlled hypertension and takes amlodipine. She does not take any other regular medications. During port insertion, her blood pressure increases from 125/75 mmHg to 185/95 mmHg. Her pulse rate is 95/min, SpO2 98% on mechanical ventilation, and end-tidal carbon dioxide 40 mmHg (35-45). The depth of anaesthesia monitor shows adequate sedation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents intraoperative hypertension during a stimulating part of the surgery (port insertion). While the patient has a history of hypertension, the acute rise in blood pressure is more likely due to inadequate analgesia during surgical stimulation given the timing. Even though patients under general anaesthesia are unconscious and unaware, they can still mount autonomic responses to painful stimuli. This nociceptive response results in sympathetic activation, leading to increased heart rate and blood pressure. Importantly, this autonomic response can be effectively controlled with adequate analgesia. This is especially important in patients with cardiac disease, who can suffer from acute cardiac ischaemia or failure with increased heart rate and blood pressure.

Fentanyl is the most appropriate choice as it directly addresses the likely cause - pain from surgical stimulation. Opioid analgesia will both relieve pain and help control the sympathetic response to surgical stimulation, thereby reducing blood pressure by blunting the autonomic response.

Labetalol is a combined alpha and beta-blocker that can effectively lower blood pressure, but it does not address the underlying cause of pain and sympathetic stimulation. It would be more appropriate if the hypertension persisted despite adequate analgesia.

Hydralazine is a direct vasodilator that can be used for hypertension but has a relatively slow onset and long duration of action, making it less suitable for acute intraoperative use. Additionally, it does not address the likely cause of the hypertension.

Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting beta-blocker that can be useful for managing acute hypertension and tachycardia, but again, treating pain should be the first step before considering antihypertensive medications.

Propofol could deepen anaesthesia and may help reduce blood pressure through its sympatholytic effects. However, this patient appears to be appropriately sedated on monitoring - it is analgesia, not sedation that is required. Propofol is not the drug of choice for analgesic effects.

When managing intraoperative hypertension, it is essential to consider common causes such as inadequate analgesia, light anaesthesia, full bladder, and hypercarbia before initiating antihypertensive therapy. The depth of anaesthesia is adequate in this case, and the end-tidal CO2 is normal, making pain the most likely cause.
learningPoints: Pain is a common and important cause of intraoperative hypertension that should be considered before initiating antihypertensive therapy.
Adequate analgesia can effectively manage hypertension caused by surgical stimulation by controlling the autonomic response.
Patients under general anaesthesia can still mount autonomic responses to painful stimuli despite being unconscious.
references: Gill R, Goldstein S. Evaluation and Management of Perioperative Hypertension. [Updated 2023 Aug 7]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557830/

Varon J, Marik PE. Perioperative hypertension management. Vasc Health Risk Manag. 2008;4(3):615-27. doi: 10.2147/vhrm.s2471. PMID: 18827911; PMCID: PMC2515421. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2515421/

Koutsaki M, Patoulias D, Tsinivizov P, Doumas M, Kallistratos M, Thomopoulos C, Poulimenos L, Agnelli G, Mancia G, Manolis A. Evaluation, risk stratification and management of hypertensive patients in the perioperative period. Eur J Intern Med. 2019 Nov;69:1-7. doi: 10.1016/j.ejim.2019.09.012. Epub 2019 Oct 9. PMID: 31606306. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31606306/
